StackSafe — Git & GitHub Lab

The whole picture

Your StackSafe Journey

Five Acts. One growing project. Every Git skill your security work will depend on.

Git and GitHub are the tools almost every software team uses to save, share, and protect their work. Mistakes here are also one of the most common real-world causes of security incidents: leaked passwords, exposed source code, work that quietly disappears.

This course follows StackSafe, a small project built by three students: Alex, Priya, and Sam. You'll do exactly what they do, real commands, in a real GitHub repository, inside your own Codespace. Every mistake below is one you'll make safely, on purpose, so you know how to prevent and fix it for real.

Each Act below builds on the last: saving and recovering work, branching and merging, handling a leaked secret, staying in sync with a shared project, and finally proposing your own change for review. The map further down shows exactly where today's session fits.

You've already been through part of this journey. Every Act marked ✓ Done on the map below is one you've completed, today picks up exactly where you left off, same story, same repository.

If anything from a previous session feels fuzzy, the Reference tab above has quick definitions for every term covered so far, no need to backtrack through the whole story to find them.

The journey, five Acts, follow Alex, Priya, and Sam as their project grows 👇

The story

Welcome to StackSafe

Three students. One project. You'll learn Git the same way they do, by making real mistakes in it.

They're building StackSafe. It's a tiny website with just a welcome message. It's simple on purpose, so every lesson stays about Git, not about the code.

Like any real team, they'll run into trouble: lost work, a secret shared by accident, two people building the same project at once. You'll work through each problem yourself, in your own copy of their project.

Alex
Starts the project
Priya
Grows a new branch
Sam
Almost leaks a secret

Tap a card below to flip it and learn what each idea means 👇 (you can also open Reference above anytime)

In one sentence: Git is the tool that saves versions. GitHub is the website where those versions are shared with a team. Codespaces is where you'll do the work.

These images show my own account name in a few places (like "mrostamide"). When you do this yourself, you'll see your own GitHub username there instead. That's correct, ignore the name, just follow the buttons and steps.

What you need before starting

A GitHub account

Free, created before this session.

A web browser

That's it. No downloads, nothing to install.

Starting your Codespace

Go to the StackSafe repository

Your instructor will give you this link: [REPO LINK]

Click the green "Code" button

You'll also notice the word "main" on this page, the name of the project's primary timeline. We'll explain this properly in Act 2, for now, just know it's the default and correct one to use.

Open Codespaces

Click the "Codespaces" tab, then click "Create codespace on main."

Why your own name matters here: later, everyone in the class will propose this same kind of change to the real shared project. If everyone wrote the identical line, only one person's change could actually be accepted, the rest would conflict with it. Since yours is personalized, your instructor can merge everyone's contribution without them colliding.

You'll see two tabs here, "Local" and "Codespaces." We're using Codespaces only, ignore "Local" for this course.

Wait for it to load

Wait a minute or so. If it feels slow, that's normal, don't refresh the page.

Plain words: This screen is the "full computer in the cloud" we just described. Git is already installed. You do not need to install anything.
You are opening my copy of StackSafe, not your own, yet. At some point soon, when Git needs to save your changes, GitHub will quietly offer to make your own copy of StackSafe, called a fork. You don't need to do anything extra except confirm it when asked, just know this is expected, not an error.

Important: saving your quota

Your free GitHub account gives you a limited number of hours per month for Codespaces. To not waste it:

  • When you finish a session, stop your codespace (do not just close the browser tab)
  • Do not delete your codespace between sessions, we will use the same one
  • If you see a message about running out of free time, tell your instructor right away

How to stop your codespace

Option A, from inside the Codespace:

  1. Press Ctrl+Shift+P (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+Shift+P (Mac)
  2. Type "Stop Current Codespace" and press Enter

Option B, from the GitHub website (easier for beginners):

  1. Go to github.com/codespaces
  2. Find your codespace in the list
  3. Click the three-dot menu (⋯) next to it
  4. Click "Stop codespace"

Don't worry, stopping does not delete your work. It just pauses the environment so it stops using your free hours.

Quick concept check

Two short questions before we start, just to make sure the big ideas landed.

1. What best describes Git?
2. While you're working in a Codespace, where does your actual project live?

Quick check before we continue

Act 1 · The story

Alex Starts Alone

Every project starts with a single save. Yours starts now.

"The plan is simple," Alex says. "One webpage. One welcome message. How hard can that be?"

Alex writes the code and saves it with Git. But there's a small mistake hiding in it.

You'll find it and fix it the same way Alex does, using your own Codespace.

Open your Codespace

If this is your first time today, or you don't have a Codespace yet: go to the StackSafe repo, click the green Code button, then Codespaces, then Create codespace on main.

If you already started one earlier and stopped it, open the same one again, not a new one: go to github.com/codespaces, find your StackSafe codespace, and click its name to resume.

Plain words: Opening it again is like waking up your computer from sleep. Everything you did before is still there. Wait a minute for it to resume, or load for the first time.

Create the file

The project folder is empty except for README.md and .devcontainer. Create a new file called index.html (right-click the StackSafe folder → New File).

Paste this inside it:

<h1>Wolcome to StackSaef</h1>

Save the file (Ctrl+S / Cmd+S).

Check on the new file

Run:

git status
Predict: what do you think Git will say about index.html?
🔒 Answer the question above to continue.
It's a command you'll run constantly. It answers one question: "What has changed since my last save point?" It doesn't change or save anything, it's just a check-up, completely safe to run anytime.

Git only knows about files after you tell it to track them. A brand-new file is called untracked.

Saving your work: two steps, not one

Saving a file in Git always takes two commands: git add → choose what you want to save, and git commit → actually save it.

Why two steps? Sometimes you change several files, but only want to save some of them right now. add lets you pick exactly what goes into your next save point. This two-step habit stays the same for the rest of the course.

Tell Git to track the file

git add index.html
Predict: run git status again. What changed?
🔒 Answer the question above to continue.

Save the file for real

git commit -m "Add first version of index.html"

Right after this, you may see a message about not having write access, and an offer to create a fork, your own copy of StackSafe.

What to do: type y and press Enter.

Two new words: origin = your fork, your own copy. upstream = the original StackSafe repo everyone shares. We'll use both again in Act 4. This is safe and expected, everything you've done so far is still saved.

Notice what you just did: you chose the file first (add), then saved it (commit). That's the pattern, every time, for the rest of this course.

Start the live preview

Right-click on index.html, then click "Open with Live Server."

If it doesn't open automatically, go to the Ports tab, find port 5500, and click the preview icon.

See the mistake, live

Plain words: Now you see it the way a visitor to the website would, clearly wrong.

Fix the mistake

Edit index.html:

<h1>Welcome to StackSafe</h1>

Save the file.

Predict: you made this change but did NOT run git add yet. What will git status show?
🔒 Answer the question above to continue.

You may also see "Your branch is ahead of 'origin/main' by 1 commit." This just means you have a save point GitHub doesn't have yet, because you haven't pushed it. Normal, ignore it for now, we'll cover pushing in Act 4.

Save the fix

git add index.html
git commit -m "Fix typo: Welcome to StackSafe"

Same two steps as before, add, then commit.

Check the preview again

Live Server should refresh automatically. The heading should now correctly read "Welcome to StackSafe."

Look at the history

git log
Plain words: git log shows every commit ever made in this project, not just yours. You'll see a few older setup commits at the bottom, made by your instructor. Your own commits appear at the top, newest first.

One more thing: this output can be long, you'll see (END) at the bottom. Press q to exit and get your terminal back.

Break it, then undo it

Now you'll see Git's real power. On purpose, delete the entire heading line in index.html, so the file is empty. Save the file.

Predict: you have NOT committed this deletion. Is the old heading still saved somewhere in Git?
🔒 Answer the question above to reveal how to get it back.

Run this to look at what Git has saved, without changing your file:

git show HEAD:index.html

Plain words: HEAD means "my last save point." This asks Git to show what the file looked like then, without touching your current file.

Now bring it back for real. Run:

git checkout -- index.html
This is the main idea of the whole tutorial: Git is a time machine. As long as you commit your work, you can never really lose it.

Mini Quiz

1. What is the correct order of steps to save a change in Git?
2. A file that Git has never seen before is called:
3. True or False: if you commit your code, and later delete it by accident, you can get it back.
CLEARED ✓
ACT 1 · COMPLETE
✨ Skills unlocked
✓ Staging & committing ✓ Reading git status ✓ Recovering with git show
Pass all three Mini Quiz questions to unlock Act 2.
Great work! End of this session.
Reference

Quick Glossary

Open this anytime, from any Act. Looking it up never affects your progress or locks.

Git

Saves versions of your project. Works offline.

GitHub

The website where Git projects are shared with a team.

Repository

A project folder Git is watching. Also called a "repo."

Codespaces

A ready-made computer in your browser. Nothing to install.

The terminal

Where you type commands and press Enter to run them.

Staging area

Where you collect exactly what you want to save next, using git add.

Commit

A permanent save point in Git's history. Made with git commit.

Fork

Your own personal copy of someone else's repository on GitHub.

Branch

A separate copy of the timeline, a safe space to try something without touching main.

Merge

Folding one branch's changes into another. Done with git merge.

.gitignore

Tells Git to never track certain files, but only from now on, not the past.

API key

A private password proving your program is allowed to use a service.

Rotate (a key)

Replacing an exposed credential with a new one. The real fix after a leak.
